bins.name: No nationwide jurisdiction basis
bins.desc: Entergy argues FERC lacks the evidentiary basis for a nationwide rule because no substantial evidence shows large load interconnections in MISO or the Entergy Operating Companies' territories are unjust or discriminatory. Regional differences mean complaints from one RTO cannot be generalized nationally. State and retail regulators retain authority over retail supply arrangements.
quotes: the evidence here does not support Commission action on a nationwide basis
the Commission cannot automatically extrapolate specific claims made about load interconnections in one area to other parts of the country

there is no evidence that would support a finding that load interconnections in MISO generally, or the service territories of the Entergy Operating Companies specifically, are unjust, unreasonable, or unduly discriminatory or preferential

the Commission should not adopt the requests of some Commenters asking the Commission to adopt standardized rules across the country and to require that such rules be included in pro forma procedures that would be filed with the Commission
the Commission should not use a new load interconnection rule to substitute its judgment for that of state regulators about what constitutes reliable retail supply arrangements

bins.name: 100 MW threshold preferred
bins.desc: Entergy opposes the proposed 20 MW threshold as too low, arguing it would sweep in speculative projects and dilute any rule's effectiveness. It recommends 100 MW and notes broad commenter consensus that 75 to 100 MW is appropriate. It also rejects voltage-based proxies as introducing unnecessary delay and complexity.
quotes: any threshold lower than 75-100 MW is more likely to capture speculative projects and could result in a Final Rule so broad that it would render it ineffective and reduce or eliminate any efficiencies that would otherwise be gained
the consensus among commentors is that increasing the threshold above 20 MW, to an amount of 75 MW-100 MW, would suffice

bins.name: Hybrid study must cover full gross load
bins.desc: Entergy contends that studying hybrid facilities on a net-injection basis alone creates reliability risks by ignoring peak gross demand when co-located generation is unavailable. It also warns that broadening the hybrid definition to include storage or demand response adds complexity requiring more study time before any expedited treatment is appropriate.
quotes: Studying only a hypothetical "net" injection or withdrawal, without considering the system effects of the hybrid load demanding up to its full gross level of demand (when, for example, its co-located generating facility is unavailable), would create unreasonable reliability risks.
it is premature to assume that the breadth of hybrid options proposed by various commenters should all be entitled to expedited interconnection study or that expedited study is feasible

bins.name: Backstop for stalled processes only
bins.desc: Entergy supports a limited federal backstop that gives large loads procedural recourse only when state processes stall for an unreasonable time, preserving flexibility for transmission providers to tailor studies to their systems. It opposes extending expedited treatment broadly to hybrid facilities without adequate reliability analysis.
quotes: the Commission should consider establishing a backstop role, so that if load interconnection procedures are stalled for an unreasonable period of time, a large load has clear procedural recourse
A backstop rule would also enable transmission providers to exercise necessary flexibility and judgment in formulating the studies and procedures that are best suited to their systems.
it is premature to assume that the breadth of hybrid options proposed by various commenters should all be entitled to expedited interconnection study or that expedited study is feasible

bins.name: Grandfathering and transition time
bins.desc: Entergy calls on FERC to affirmatively confirm that in-progress and existing agreements are grandfathered, noting broad commenter consensus on this point. It also urges a significant transition period before any new rule takes effect, with the length tied to the final rule's scope.
quotes: the Commission should affirmatively ensure no retroactive impact on in-process or existing agreements
A significant transition period will be necessary for states, stakeholders and transmission owners to adjust to a new regulatory scheme.

bins.name: No standardized study queue
bins.desc: Entergy opposes both a standard load interconnection queue and a combined generator-load queue, arguing a combined queue would worsen existing generator queue delays and disrupt ongoing reforms. It also opposes mandatory nationwide pro forma study procedures.
quotes: the Commission should not adopt the requests of some Commenters asking the Commission to adopt standardized rules across the country and to require that such rules be included in pro forma procedures that would be filed with the Commission
the Commission should not establish a standard queue for load interconnections
a combined queue likely would lead to delays in processing interconnection requests even beyond those that would arise from a standardized load interconnection queue

Studying only a hypothetical "net" injection or withdrawal, without considering the system effects of the hybrid load demanding up to its full gross level of demand (when, for example, its co-located generating facility is unavailable), would create unreasonable reliability risks.
it is premature to assume that the breadth of hybrid options proposed by various commenters should all be entitled to expedited interconnection study or that expedited study is feasible

bins.name: State authority over flexible loads
bins.desc: Entergy argues that demand response and curtailability are retail supply matters under state jurisdiction, not federal interconnection issues. FERC should not use a new load interconnection rule to override state decisions on reliable retail supply arrangements.
quotes: the Commission should not use a new load interconnection rule to substitute its judgment for that of state regulators about what constitutes reliable retail supply arrangements

bins.name: MISO processes working well
bins.desc: Entergy highlights that MISO's existing large load interconnection processes are functioning well and that its own service territories have successfully interconnected major data centers for Google, Meta, and AWS under efficient state regulatory frameworks.
quotes: the Commission cannot automatically extrapolate specific claims made about load interconnections in one area to other parts of the country

there is no evidence that would support a finding that load interconnections in MISO generally, or the service territories of the Entergy Operating Companies specifically, are unjust, unreasonable, or unduly discriminatory or preferential

bins.name: PJM issues are PJM-specific
bins.desc: Entergy takes no position on PJM-specific claims but cautions that problems documented for PJM or individual transmission owners there cannot be extrapolated to other regions.
quotes: the Commission cannot automatically extrapolate specific claims made about load interconnections in one area to other parts of the country

bins.name: Substantial evidence requirement
bins.desc: Entergy emphasizes that FERC may only mandate new interconnection rules under FPA Section 206 upon a finding of substantial evidence that existing rates or conditions are unjust, unreasonable, or unduly discriminatory. Unsupported allegations and regional anecdotes from other areas do not meet that standard.
quotes: the evidence here does not support Commission action on a nationwide basis
the Commission cannot automatically extrapolate specific claims made about load interconnections in one area to other parts of the country

there is no evidence that would support a finding that load interconnections in MISO generally, or the service territories of the Entergy Operating Companies specifically, are unjust, unreasonable, or unduly discriminatory or preferential

the Commission should not adopt the requests of some Commenters asking the Commission to adopt standardized rules across the country and to require that such rules be included in pro forma procedures that would be filed with the Commission

bins.name: Limited federal backstop rule
bins.desc: Entergy recommends FERC establish a backstop role providing procedural recourse when state-level processes stall, rather than imposing nationwide standardized procedures. This approach aligns with cooperative federalism principles and the DOE's stated goal of smoothing interconnection paths.
quotes: the Commission should consider establishing a backstop role, so that if load interconnection procedures are stalled for an unreasonable period of time, a large load has clear procedural recourse
A backstop rule would also enable transmission providers to exercise necessary flexibility and judgment in formulating the studies and procedures that are best suited to their systems.

bins.name: Separate load and generator queues
bins.desc: Entergy opposes both a standardized load queue and any combined generator-load queue. A combined queue would add to existing generator queue backlogs and disrupt ongoing interconnection reforms. Load and generation have separate effects on the bulk electric system and should be studied separately.
quotes: the Commission should not establish a standard queue for load interconnections
a combined queue likely would lead to delays in processing interconnection requests even beyond those that would arise from a standardized load interconnection queue

bins.name: Reliability first priority
bins.desc: Entergy urges FERC to reaffirm that reliability is the first priority for bulk electric system interconnections. Net-injection-only studies for hybrid facilities and premature expedited treatment of broadly defined hybrid resources both pose reliability risks that warrant caution.
quotes: Studying only a hypothetical "net" injection or withdrawal, without considering the system effects of the hybrid load demanding up to its full gross level of demand (when, for example, its co-located generating facility is unavailable), would create unreasonable reliability risks.
it is premature to assume that the breadth of hybrid options proposed by various commenters should all be entitled to expedited interconnection study or that expedited study is feasible

bins.name: State authority over retail supply
bins.desc: Entergy argues that demand response, curtailment, and flexible load arrangements are retail service matters under state jurisdiction. FERC should not use a load interconnection rule to displace state regulatory decisions about reliable retail supply in bundled-service territories.
quotes: the Commission should not use a new load interconnection rule to substitute its judgment for that of state regulators about what constitutes reliable retail supply arrangements

bins.name: Protect in-progress agreements
bins.desc: Entergy cites broad commenter consensus that existing and in-progress interconnection agreements must be grandfathered under any final rule. Projects with significant financial, contractual, and engineering commitments should not be disrupted, and a meaningful transition period is needed.
quotes: the Commission should affirmatively ensure no retroactive impact on in-process or existing agreements
A significant transition period will be necessary for states, stakeholders and transmission owners to adjust to a new regulatory scheme.
